title: Themes, Styles & Dark Mode
description: View system theming, Compose MaterialTheme, custom design systems, dark mode, and Material You dynamic colors
lesson:
View System: Styles vs Themes

In the View system, a style and a theme are both defined with <style> in styles.xml, but they serve different purposes:

StyleTheme
Applied toA single View via style="@style/..."An Activity or Application via android:theme="@style/..."
ScopeOnly that view's attributesEntire subtree — all child views inherit it
DefinesConcrete values: textSize=16spAbstract attributes: colorPrimary=#6200EE
Example<style name="CardTitle"><style name="AppTheme" parent="Theme.Material3.DayNight">
<!-- Style: applied to a single view --> <style name="Widget.MyApp.Title"> <item name="android:textSize">20sp</item> <item name="android:textColor">?attr/colorOnSurface</item> </style> <!-- Theme: applied to Activity or Application --> <style name="Theme.MyApp" parent="Theme.Material3.DayNight.NoActionBar"> <item name="colorPrimary">@color/purple_500</item> <item name="colorSecondary">@color/teal_200</item> <item name="colorSurface">@color/white</item> </style>
Key insight: A style says "this button looks like X." A theme says "in this part of the app, primary color means purple, surface means white." Styles consume theme attributes; themes define them.

Theme Inheritance Chain

Android themes follow a clear inheritance chain. Each generation adds Material Design features:

Theme.AppCompat (v7) │ Basic Material Design backport, Toolbar, etc. │ ▼ Theme.MaterialComponents (MDC 1.x) │ Material Design 2: FAB, BottomNav, Snackbar, │ shape theming, type scales │ ▼ Theme.Material3 (MDC 1.5+) │ Material Design 3 / Material You: │ Dynamic Color, tonal palettes, updated components │ └── Your App Theme <style name="Theme.MyApp" parent="Theme.Material3.DayNight.NoActionBar">

Always extend the latest generation you can support. Theme.Material3.DayNight gives you automatic light/dark switching and access to all M3 color roles (primary, secondary, tertiary, surface, error, and their on/container variants).

Theme Attributes: ?attr/ References

The ?attr/ syntax is how XML layouts reference the current theme's values instead of hardcoding colors. This is the backbone of themeable UI:

<!-- WRONG: hardcoded color — breaks in dark mode --> <TextView android:textColor="#000000" /> <!-- RIGHT: resolves from current theme --> <TextView android:textColor="?attr/colorOnSurface" /> <!-- Common theme attributes: --> ?attr/colorPrimary — brand color ?attr/colorOnPrimary — text/icon on primary ?attr/colorSurface — background of cards, sheets ?attr/colorOnSurface — text/icon on surface ?attr/colorError — error states ?attr/colorSurfaceVariant — secondary surfaces
Never hardcode colors in layouts. Always use ?attr/ references. This is what makes dark mode, dynamic color, and per-Activity theming work — the same layout resolves to different colors depending on the active theme.

Compose Theming: MaterialTheme

In Compose, theming replaces XML with Kotlin. MaterialTheme is a composable that provides three pillars via CompositionLocal:

PillarTypeAccess Via
ColorsColorSchemeMaterialTheme.colorScheme
TypographyTypographyMaterialTheme.typography
ShapesShapesMaterialTheme.shapes
// MaterialTheme wraps your app content MaterialTheme( colorScheme = LightColorScheme, typography = AppTypography, shapes = AppShapes ) { // All children can access theme values Surface(color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.background) { Text( text = "Hello", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onBackground ) } }

Creating a Custom Compose Theme

The standard pattern: define light and dark ColorSchemes, detect system dark mode, and wrap in a theme composable:

private val LightColors = lightColorScheme( primary = Color(0xFF6200EE), onPrimary = Color.White, secondary = Color(0xFF03DAC6), surface = Color.White, onSurface = Color.Black, background = Color(0xFFFFFBFE), onBackground = Color.Black ) private val DarkColors = darkColorScheme( primary = Color(0xFFBB86FC), onPrimary = Color.Black, secondary = Color(0xFF03DAC6), surface = Color(0xFF1C1B1F), onSurface = Color.White, background = Color(0xFF1C1B1F), onBackground = Color.White ) @Composable fun MyAppTheme( darkTheme: Boolean = isSystemInDarkTheme(), content: @Composable () -> Unit ) { val colorScheme = if (darkTheme) DarkColors else LightColors MaterialTheme( colorScheme = colorScheme, typography = AppTypography, shapes = AppShapes, content = content ) }

isSystemInDarkTheme() reads the system setting. When the user toggles dark mode, Compose recomposes with the new color scheme — no Activity restart needed.

Accessing Theme Values in Compose

The Compose equivalent of ?attr/ is accessing properties on the MaterialTheme object:

// Colors val primary = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary val onSurface = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurface val errorColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.error // Typography val headlineStyle = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineLarge val bodyStyle = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyMedium val labelStyle = MaterialTheme.typography.labelSmall // Shapes val cardShape = MaterialTheme.shapes.medium // RoundedCornerShape(12.dp) val chipShape = MaterialTheme.shapes.small // RoundedCornerShape(8.dp) // Usage in a composable Card( colors = CardDefaults.cardColors( containerColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surfaceVariant ), shape = MaterialTheme.shapes.medium ) { Text( text = "Card Title", style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleLarge, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurfaceVariant ) }

Custom Design Systems with CompositionLocal

When Material3's color roles don't fit your brand, you can extend the theme with custom properties using CompositionLocal. This is how production apps implement design systems:

// 1. Define your custom color properties data class BrandColors( val accent: Color = Color.Unspecified, val warning: Color = Color.Unspecified, val gradient: List<Color> = emptyList(), val cardHighlight: Color = Color.Unspecified ) // 2. Create a CompositionLocal to hold them val LocalBrandColors = staticCompositionLocalOf { BrandColors() } // 3. Provide values in your theme @Composable fun MyAppTheme( darkTheme: Boolean = isSystemInDarkTheme(), content: @Composable () -> Unit ) { val brandColors = if (darkTheme) { BrandColors( accent = Color(0xFFFF6B6B), warning = Color(0xFFFFD93D), cardHighlight = Color(0xFF2D2D3F) ) } else { BrandColors( accent = Color(0xFFE63946), warning = Color(0xFFF4A261), cardHighlight = Color(0xFFF1FAEE) ) } CompositionLocalProvider(LocalBrandColors provides brandColors) { MaterialTheme( colorScheme = if (darkTheme) DarkColors else LightColors, content = content ) } } // 4. Access via extension property object MyAppTheme { val brandColors: BrandColors @Composable get() = LocalBrandColors.current } // 5. Use in composables Box(Modifier.background(MyAppTheme.brandColors.accent))
Pattern: Material3 is the base — you extend it with CompositionLocals for brand-specific tokens. You still get all Material3 components and can override their defaults. This is how large apps like Twitter/X and Spotify implement design systems in Compose.

Dark Mode Implementation

View System: DayNight Theme

For the View system, dark mode is built on resource qualifiers:

res/ ├── values/ │ ├── colors.xml ← light mode colors │ └── themes.xml ← parent: Theme.Material3.DayNight └── values-night/ └── colors.xml ← dark mode overrides When system is in dark mode, Android loads values-night/ resources automatically — no code changes needed (if you used ?attr/ everywhere)
<!-- res/values/colors.xml (light) --> <color name="surface">#FFFBFE</color> <color name="on_surface">#1C1B1F</color> <!-- res/values-night/colors.xml (dark) --> <color name="surface">#1C1B1F</color> <color name="on_surface">#E6E1E5</color>

Forcing Dark Mode Programmatically

// Force dark mode regardless of system setting AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode(AppCompatDelegate.MODE_NIGHT_YES) // Force light mode AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode(AppCompatDelegate.MODE_NIGHT_NO) // Follow system (default behavior) AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode(AppCompatDelegate.MODE_NIGHT_FOLLOW_SYSTEM) // Typically called in Application.onCreate() or Settings screen // This recreates all Activities to apply the new theme

Compose: isSystemInDarkTheme()

In Compose, dark mode is just a branch in your theme composable — no resource qualifiers needed:

@Composable fun MyAppTheme(content: @Composable () -> Unit) { val colorScheme = when { // API 31+ dynamic color from wallpaper Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= 31 -> { val context = LocalContext.current if (isSystemInDarkTheme()) dynamicDarkColorScheme(context) else dynamicLightColorScheme(context) } // Fallback: static color schemes isSystemInDarkTheme() -> DarkColors else -> LightColors } MaterialTheme(colorScheme = colorScheme, content = content) }

Material You / Dynamic Colors (API 31+)

Starting with Android 12, Material You generates a color scheme from the user's wallpaper. The system extracts seed colors and generates tonal palettes:

User's Wallpaper │ ▼ Color Extraction (system) │ ▼ Seed Color (dominant hue) │ ├── Primary tonal palette (13 shades) ├── Secondary tonal palette (13 shades) ├── Tertiary tonal palette (13 shades) ├── Neutral tonal palette (13 shades) └── Neutral variant palette (13 shades) │ ▼ dynamicLightColorScheme(context) / dynamicDarkColorScheme(context) Maps tonal palette shades → Material3 color roles
// Dynamic color in Compose (API 31+) val dynamicColor = Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S val colorScheme = when { dynamicColor && darkTheme -> dynamicDarkColorScheme(context) dynamicColor && !darkTheme -> dynamicLightColorScheme(context) darkTheme -> DarkColors else -> LightColors } // For View system: apply in theme // DynamicColors.applyToActivitiesIfAvailable(application)
Gotcha: Dynamic color is only available on API 31+ (Pixel, Samsung One UI 4+, most OEMs). Always provide a fallback static color scheme for older devices. Also note: some OEMs (Xiaomi, Oppo) may not expose wallpaper colors the same way — test on real devices.

DataBinding vs ViewBinding in Theme Context

ViewBindingDataBinding
What it doesType-safe access to views (replaces findViewById)Binds data directly in XML with <layout> + expressions
Theme relevanceNone — just view referencesCan reference theme attrs in binding expressions
Compose equivalentNot needed — composables ARE the viewsNot needed — state hoisting replaces data binding
Current statusStill useful in View-based codeEffectively dead — Compose state + recomposition replaced it
Interview tip: DataBinding had XML expressions like @{viewModel.name} and two-way binding with @={}. It was powerful but added build complexity (annotation processing, generated binding classes). In Compose, State + recomposition gives you the same reactive UI without any codegen. DataBinding is not recommended for new projects.

questions.title: Style vs Theme
questions.description: What is the fundamental difference between a style and a theme in the Android View system?
options: A style uses XML, a theme uses Kotlin code
A style is applied to a single View; a theme is applied to an Activity or Application and affects all child views
A theme can only define colors, a style can define any attribute
There is no difference — they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Both are defined with <style> in XML, but a style is applied to a single view via style="@style/..." and sets concrete attributes. A theme is applied to an Activity or Application via android:theme="@style/..." and defines abstract attributes (like colorPrimary) that all descendant views can reference with ?attr/.
hints: Think about scope — how many views does each affect?
Consider how you apply each one in XML

questions.title: Theme Attribute References
questions.description: In a View-based layout XML, what does android:textColor="?attr/colorOnSurface" do?
options: Sets the text color to a hardcoded value named colorOnSurface
Looks up the value of colorOnSurface from the currently applied theme at runtime
References a color resource file named colorOnSurface.xml
Applies the colorOnSurface style to the text view
questions.explanation: The ?attr/ prefix tells Android to resolve the value from the current theme at runtime. If the theme defines colorOnSurface as black (light mode) or white (dark mode), the same layout automatically adapts. This indirection is what makes theming, dark mode, and per-Activity theme overrides possible without duplicating layouts.
hints: The ? prefix means something is resolved dynamically, not statically
What changes when the user switches between light and dark mode?

questions.title: Compose Theme Selection
questions.description: What does this theme composable do, and what color scheme will be used on a Pixel 7 running Android 14 in dark mode?
options: Always uses static DarkColors because darkTheme is true
Uses dynamicDarkColorScheme — wallpaper-derived dark colors — because Pixel 7 is API 33 (>= 31) and system is in dark mode
Crashes because dynamicDarkColorScheme is not available on API 33
Uses dynamicLightColorScheme because light is the default
questions.explanation: Pixel 7 runs Android 13 (API 33), which is >= 31, so the first branch matches. Since isSystemInDarkTheme() returns true (dark mode), it uses dynamicDarkColorScheme(context). This extracts the user's wallpaper colors and generates a dark tonal palette — the colors are personalized to each user's wallpaper. The static DarkColors are only used as a fallback for API < 31.
hints: Check the API level of the Pixel 7 — is it >= 31?
Two conditions are checked: API level first, then dark mode

questions.title: Custom CompositionLocal Theme Extension
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a custom spacing system that extends MaterialTheme using CompositionLocal.

blanks.hint: Creates a CompositionLocal that rarely changes (more efficient than dynamic)
The composable that provides values to CompositionLocals

questions.explanation: staticCompositionLocalOf creates a CompositionLocal optimized for values that rarely change (theme values typically don't change during a session). CompositionLocalProvider is the composable that injects the value into the composition tree. Any descendant composable reads it via LocalAppSpacing.current. This pattern lets you extend MaterialTheme with any custom design tokens your app needs.
hints: There are two kinds of CompositionLocal: static (rarely changes) and dynamic (changes often)
The Provider composable wraps the subtree that needs access

questions.title: Dark Mode Not Working in Views
questions.description: This app has a dark mode toggle in settings, but changing it has no effect. The theme never actually switches. Fix the implementation.

checks.message: Use AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode() to switch dark mode

questions.explanation: Calling setTheme() on an Activity does NOT trigger a configuration change — it only affects newly inflated views. The correct API is AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode(), which triggers a configuration change and causes all Activities to recreate. The theme must extend DayNight so Android automatically loads values-night/ resources when night mode is active. You provide dark overrides in res/values-night/colors.xml rather than maintaining separate theme definitions.
hints: setTheme() only affects views inflated AFTER the call — existing views don't update
There's an AppCompat API specifically designed for switching between day and night modes

questions.title: MaterialTheme Pillars in Compose
questions.description: Name the three pillars of MaterialTheme in Compose and explain how you access each one's values inside a composable.
questions.answer: The three pillars are: (1) ColorScheme — accessed via MaterialTheme.colorScheme (e.g., MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary, .onSurface, .surfaceVariant). (2) Typography — accessed via MaterialTheme.typography (e.g., MaterialTheme.typography.headlineLarge, .bodyMedium, .labelSmall). (3) Shapes — accessed via MaterialTheme.shapes (e.g., MaterialTheme.shapes.small, .medium, .large). Under the hood, each pillar is provided via a CompositionLocal, and MaterialTheme is just a convenience composable that sets all three at once.
questions.explanation: These three pillars map to Material Design 3's core design tokens: color, type, and shape. By accessing them through MaterialTheme rather than hardcoding values, your composables automatically adapt when the theme changes (dark mode, dynamic color, or per-screen theming). The composable reads the nearest provided value from the composition tree, just like ?attr/ in the View system.
hints: Think about the three visual aspects that define how a UI looks
Each pillar has its own accessor on the MaterialTheme object

questions.title: Theme Inheritance
questions.description: Your app uses Theme.MaterialComponents.DayNight and you want to adopt Material Design 3 with dynamic color support. What should you change the parent theme to?
options: Theme.AppCompat.DayNight — it has the widest device compatibility
Theme.Material3.DayNight — the M3 successor that supports dynamic color
No change needed — MaterialComponents already supports Material 3
questions.explanation: Theme.Material3.DayNight is the correct parent for Material Design 3 apps. It extends MaterialComponents with M3 color roles (primary, secondary, tertiary, surface, and their on/container variants), updated component styles, and support for dynamic color via DynamicColors.applyToActivitiesIfAvailable(). There is no DynamicColor suffix — dynamic color is applied programmatically, not via theme inheritance.
hints: Material Design versions map to theme parent names: M2 → MaterialComponents, M3 → Material3
Dynamic color is an API call, not a theme variant

questions.title: Complete Compose Theme with Dark Mode + Dynamic Color
questions.description: Write a complete MyAppTheme composable that: (1) uses dynamic color on API 31+, (2) falls back to static light/dark color schemes on older APIs, (3) respects system dark mode setting. Assume LightColors and DarkColors are already defined.

checks.message: Check API level for dynamic color support
Use dynamic color scheme functions for API 31+
Apply the selected color scheme via MaterialTheme

questions.explanation: This is the standard Compose theme pattern used by the official Android project templates. The when block first checks if dynamic color is both enabled and available (API 31+), then falls back to static schemes. LocalContext.current provides the Context needed by the dynamic color scheme functions to read wallpaper colors. The darkTheme parameter defaults to isSystemInDarkTheme() but can be overridden — useful for in-app dark mode toggles or previews.
hints: Dynamic color needs two conditions: the feature flag AND API level
You need a Context to extract wallpaper colors — use LocalContext.current
The else branches handle devices that don't support dynamic color
